paragraph: Researchers at the Veldmoor Institute of Paleontological Sciences announced the discovery of a new large-bodied theropod dinosaur from the Upper Korvath Formation of central Paluvia, dated to approximately 78.4 million years ago. The species, named Lurithosaurus barovae, is represented by a partial skeleton comprising 34 bones, including a near-complete skull 1.12 meters in length, recovered from Drevenya Quarry over three field seasons between 2021 and 2024. Computed tomography scanning of the braincase revealed an endocranial volume of 312 cubic centimeters, substantially larger than comparable taxa from the same stratigraphic interval. Bite force estimates derived from finite-element modeling of the mandible reach 47,300 Newtons at the posterior dentition, placing Lurithosaurus barovae among the most powerful biters yet documented for non-avian theropods. The holotype individual, catalogued as VIPS-2024-0047, is estimated to have attained a body length of 11.7 meters and a mass of approximately 7,200 kilograms based on limb-bone scaling equations. Phylogenetic analysis incorporating 412 morphological characters resolved the new taxon as a member of the clade Veldosauridae, sister to the previously described Gorathen magnus. The findings are reported in the current issue of the Journal of Paluvia Paleontology.

paragraph: A team from the Ostrava Center for Earth History has documented an exceptionally preserved marine reptile fauna from the Brelinsk Limestone of the Morovic Basin, with radiometric ages clustering at 163.7 ± 0.4 million years ago. The assemblage, excavated at site BL-7 between 2019 and 2023, includes five partial skeletons of a new pliosaur species designated Corvethyus grandipelvis, whose pelvic girdle width of 0.94 meters indicates an organism capable of delivering powerful thrust strokes. Total body length reconstructed from proportional comparisons reaches 9.3 meters. Stable oxygen isotope analysis of tooth enamel (delta-18O values averaging +22.1 per mil VSMOW) suggests a paleotemperature of approximately 26 degrees Celsius for the depositional environment, consistent with a warm subtropical seaway. Additionally, seven specimens of the ichthyosaur Narotex brevidens were recovered from the same horizon, with snout-to-fluke lengths ranging from 2.1 to 4.6 meters, likely representing multiple ontogenetic stages. Stomach contents preserved in two individuals of Narotex brevidens include cephalopod hooklets numbering over 3,400 per specimen, providing direct dietary evidence. The study, published in Mesozoic Marine Systems, advances understanding of niche partitioning within Jurassic marine apex predator communities.

paragraph: Palynological and macrofossil evidence recovered from the Threskova Coal Measures of northern Drasmia has pushed back the earliest confirmed occurrence of angiosperm-dominated forest ecosystems to 103.2 million years ago, approximately 4.5 million years earlier than previously recognized. Investigators at the Drasmian Natural Sciences Academy collected 1,847 identifiable pollen grains and 223 leaf impressions across a continuous 14-meter stratigraphic section, with angiosperm taxa comprising 61 percent of the assemblage by the uppermost beds. A newly described tree species, Palvoria drevenyana, exhibits compound pinnate leaves up to 38 centimeters long with a distinctive reticulate venation density of 22 veins per square centimeter, interpreted as an adaptation to high-humidity closed-canopy conditions. Carbon isotope values from bulk organic matter in the same beds (delta-13C averaging -26.4 per mil VPDB) are consistent with C3 photosynthetic pathways under atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations estimated between 900 and 1,100 parts per million for the mid-Cretaceous interval. The assemblage also preserves two species of extinct fern, Rovathia spinulata and Brelinskia minor, suggesting a mixed understory community. Results appear in the Botanical Paleorecord, volume 47.

paragraph: Fieldwork conducted by the Quarvik Evolutionary Research Consortium in the badlands of the Sulenthi Depression has yielded a growth series of 19 skulls belonging to the ceratopsid dinosaur Trevakus horridus, spanning an estimated ontogenetic range from hatchling to fully mature adult. The adult specimens, averaging 1.87 meters in cranial length, display elaborate frill ornamentation with paired epoccipitals numbering up to 14, whereas juvenile skulls recovered from the same bonebed lack these structures entirely, confirming that the elaborate display apparatus was acquired post-hatching. The bonebed, designated QRC-BB-12, extends over an outcrop area of approximately 400 square meters and is interpreted as a catastrophic drought-related mass mortality horizon based on sedimentological evidence including pedogenic carbonate nodule abundance of 38 nodules per cubic meter. Histological thin sections from femoral cortices of five individuals reveal highly vascularized fibrolamellar bone consistent with rapid growth rates averaging 22 kilograms per year during the juvenile phase. Stable strontium isotope ratios (87Sr/86Sr = 0.70824 ± 0.00003) align the deposit with the Sulenthi Regional Chronostratigraphic Standard at 74.1 million years ago. The report is published in Cretaceous Vertebrate Biology.

paragraph: Scientists from the Peloria University Department of Human Origins have announced the recovery of a partial hominin cranium from the Ngomvela Tuff sequence of the East Dravinan Rift, radiometrically dated to 2.34 million years ago by argon-argon methods with an uncertainty of ±0.06 million years. The specimen, catalogued as NU-2023-CR-001, preserves the frontal, right parietal, and substantial portions of the occipital, allowing endocranial volume reconstruction via virtual filling to yield an estimate of 748 cubic centimeters, intermediate between known Pliocene australopith values and early Homo specimens from correlative horizons elsewhere. Facial prognathism index calculated from the preserved nasion-prosthion distance of 31.4 millimeters relative to basion-nasion length is notably reduced compared to Australopithecus analogues, suggesting incipient facial retraction. Dental microwear texture analysis of two recovered molars documents a high prevalence of pits relative to scratches (pit fraction 0.68), indicative of a dietary regime incorporating hard, brittle food items. Associated lithic artifacts recovered within 2 meters of the cranium include 47 flaked basalt cobbles attributed to a local mode-1 technological tradition. The findings are presented in the Journal of Human Evolutionary Studies.

paragraph: A new study from researchers at the Breltova Institute of Geobiology reports the discovery of exceptionally preserved soft-tissue traces in the Vorlisk Konservat-Lagerstate of the Pernian Shield, extending the known record of motile bilaterians to 558 million years ago. The traces, designated ichnogenus Vorliskia meanderans, consist of sinuous horizontal burrows averaging 3.2 millimeters in width and up to 47 centimeters in preserved length, produced in microbially bound seafloor sediments. Scanning electron microscopy of burrow linings reveals structured organic films with carbon-to-nitrogen ratios of 8.4, compatible with bacterial biofilm preservation rather than body tissue. Three distinct behavioral patterns are identified in the ichnofabric: straight exploratory trails, tight spiral foraging loops, and short vertical shafts interpreted as temporary escape structures. Raman spectroscopic mapping of the organic residues identifies aromatic compounds consistent with degraded chitin or collagen-like materials, suggesting the trace-maker possessed a differentiated body wall. The Vorlisk Formation directly underlies the dated Grenivka Volcanic Member, providing a maximum age constraint of 557.8 ± 0.5 million years for the trace assemblage. The research is published in Early Metazoan Records, volume 9.

paragraph: Paleontologists from the Caldrun Museum of Natural History have described Mevarius dipteryx, a new species of pterosaur from the Sulkov Beds of the Trebian Plateau, representing the largest flying vertebrate yet documented from the Late Cretaceous of that region. The wingspan, reconstructed from a complete right wing metacarpal measuring 68.2 centimeters in length, is estimated at 9.1 meters using regression equations calibrated against seven articulated comparators. The skull, recovered in three-dimensional preservation from a calcareous mudstone concretion, measures 1.34 meters from premaxilla to occiput and bears 22 slender, procumbent teeth in the anterior rostrum, absent in posterior regions, consistent with a piscivorous feeding strategy. Aerodynamic modeling using digitized landmark data predicts a minimum flight speed of 8.3 meters per second and a soaring glide ratio of approximately 1:17, superior to most extant large soaring birds. Bone histology from the humerus cortex reveals highly compact, avascular tissue interpreted as consistent with the physiological demands of sustained dynamic soaring. Associated trace fossils preserve probable pterosaur footprints with digit IV impressions measuring 14 centimeters, reinforcing the interpretation of a large-bodied taxon. The description appears in Volant Archosaur Reports.

paragraph: An international consortium led by the Strevka Geochemical Institute has conducted uranium-lead zircon dating of ash beds bracketing the Vosmerin Extinction Event in the Nordichev Basin, constraining this regional biotic crisis to a duration of no more than 420,000 years centered at 201.8 million years ago. The event, previously identified solely on biostratigraphic grounds, is now confirmed to coincide with a negative carbon isotope excursion of -4.6 per mil in both carbonate and organic carbon records across 17 measured sections. Calcareous nannofossil assemblage richness drops from 34 species below the event to 9 species above it in the most expanded sections, while bivalve genus richness declines by 71 percent across the same interval. Sulfur isotope data from pyrite framboids (delta-34S values shifting from +18.2 to -22.4 per mil) indicate a rapid expansion of euxinic bottom water conditions contemporaneous with the main extinction pulse. Mercury concentration anomalies reaching 480 parts per billion in organic-rich shales at four of the measured sections are interpreted as evidence of large-scale volcanic outgassing from the Torvennic Large Igneous Province, proposed as the primary extinction driver. Results are reported in Global and Planetary Change Records.

paragraph: Researchers at the Zivansk Paleontological Survey have described a new genus and species of early mammal, Tholbrix microdus, recovered from the Falventian Fissure Fill deposits of the Brelnic Highlands, dated to approximately 167.3 million years ago. The specimen consists of a complete lower jaw 18.4 millimeters in length bearing four molars with a distinctly tribosphenic cusp pattern not previously documented in Jurassic mammals from this region. Micro-computed tomography of the jaw revealed a fully enclosed Meckelian groove, indicating the dentary-squamosal jaw joint was the sole articulation, placing Tholbrix microdus within the crown mammalian radiation despite its antiquity. Molar crown height averages 1.2 millimeters, and enamel microstructure analysis shows three-dimensional prism decussation consistent with dietary processing of hard invertebrate prey. The fissure fill matrix, a karst-related red mudstone, also yielded 14 isolated teeth of indeterminate mammaliaform affinity and three lizard jaw fragments. Body mass estimate based on lower molar row length (sum = 4.8 millimeters) places Tholbrix microdus at approximately 28 grams, comparable to a small extant shrew. The findings are published in Mesozoic Mammal Research.

paragraph: A study published in Isotopic Paleoecology by scientists from the Vrentova Academy of Sciences presents strontium and neodymium isotope data from 88 fossil shark teeth recovered across 12 stratigraphic levels in the Pelkian Chalk, spanning the interval from 86.0 to 80.5 million years ago. The dataset documents a systematic shift in 87Sr/86Sr ratios from 0.70720 at the base of the study interval to 0.70758 at the top, tracking global seawater evolution and providing a high-resolution chemostratigraphic framework for the formation. Epsilon-Nd values ranging from -8.3 to -11.6 across the tooth sample set indicate variable contributions from old continental crust erosion versus juvenile volcanic arc sources to the regional water mass over this 5.5-million-year window. The taxonomic composition of the shark fauna, represented by 11 morphologically distinct tooth morphotypes, shifts notably at 83.2 million years ago, when the large-bodied predator morphotype Drevkarchas breviaxis disappears and is replaced by three smaller, more gracile morphotypes interpreted as specialized durophagous or planktivorous feeders. This faunal turnover correlates with a negative excursion in delta-18O values of 1.4 per mil, interpreted as a brief warming episode. The research contributes new calibration points for Late Cretaceous chemostratigraphy.

paragraph: The Falventian Desert Expedition, a joint project of the Ostrim University Earth Sciences Faculty and the Brelnic Desert Research Authority, has recovered the most complete skeleton yet found of the ankylosaur Turvakis densus from the Faldenic Red Beds of southern Falventia, dated to 70.2 million years ago. The tail club, measured at 47.3 centimeters maximum transverse diameter, bears a dense trabecular internal architecture confirmed by micro-CT, with a calculated resistance to torsional fracture 3.8 times greater than that of the handle vertebrae, consistent with use as an active defensive weapon. Mass estimates from volumetric body reconstructions place Turvakis densus at 4,100 kilograms. Bone surface textures on the dorsal osteoderms reveal annual growth rings in 11 specimens, yielding maximum longevity estimates of 38 years for the largest individual. Sedimentological context indicates deposition in a semi-arid floodplain setting with seasonal flooding episodes recorded by crevasse-splay sandstone lenses. Findings appear in Cretaceous Paleoecology Reports.

paragraph: Scientists at the Prevelkov Center for Marine Evolutionary Biology have used geometric morphometrics to quantify shape variation in 342 echinoid tests from a continuous stratigraphic section spanning the Droven-Velkian boundary in the Nordichev Basin, approximately 93.4 million years ago. The newly described species Korvathechinus elongatus, first appearing 93.1 million years ago, exhibits a disc-to-diameter ratio of 0.41, compared to 0.27 in its inferred ancestor Korvathechinus rotundus. Rates of morphological change, calculated as darwins, peak at 2,840 darwins during a 120,000-year interval centered on 93.0 million years ago, suggesting episodic rather than gradual change. Oxygen isotope measurements from test calcite (delta-18O = -1.8 ± 0.3 per mil) indicate a sea-surface cooling of approximately 2.1 degrees Celsius coinciding with the morphological transition, potentially driving the observed selection pressure. The results are published in Invertebrate Evolutionary Dynamics.

paragraph: Excavations by the Grinthal Fossil Research Group at the Drevelnik Bone Quarry in the Grinthal Province have uncovered a monodominant sauropod bonebed containing the disarticulated remains of at least 23 individuals of Kolibrix longaevus, a new species of titanosaur-grade sauropod. Femur lengths in the assemblage range from 0.61 to 1.94 meters, representing a near-continuous size distribution interpreted as a multi-age herd. The deposit is dated to 75.6 million years ago by magnetostratigraphic correlation to chron C33n and confirmed by detrital zircon maximum depositional ages of 75.9 ± 1.2 million years. Kolibrix longaevus adult individuals are estimated at 22 meters body length and 31,000 kilograms body mass, with neck length comprising 42 percent of total presacral vertebral column length, the highest ratio documented in this formation's sauropod fauna. Seventeen neck vertebrae preserve internal pneumatic chambers with a calculated air space proportion of 68 percent, substantially reducing skeletal mass relative to solid-bone analogues. Geochemical analysis of 44 teeth using clumped isotope thermometry (delta-47 values averaging 0.619 per mil) yields body temperature estimates of 37.6 degrees Celsius, consistent with endothermic-like thermoregulation. Findings are published in Sauropod Biology and Evolution.

paragraph: Researchers from the Velikov Institute of Quaternary Sciences have published a multiproxy paleoclimate reconstruction based on pollen, chironomid head capsules, and stable isotopes from a continuous 8.4-meter sediment core retrieved from Lake Drevelnik in the Zivansk Plateau, spanning the interval from 120,000 to 11,500 years before present. Pollen assemblage zones identified by constrained clustering delineate nine floristic phases, with tundra taxa (Rovathia pollen type) dominating from 73,000 to 14,500 years before present, achieving relative abundances exceeding 84 percent during the Last Glacial Maximum at approximately 22,000 years before present. Chironomid-inferred July air temperatures reach a minimum of 4.2 degrees Celsius at 22,400 years before present and a maximum of 17.8 degrees Celsius during the early Holocene thermal maximum at 9,200 years before present. Carbon-14 dating of 32 terrestrial plant macrofossils provides the chronological framework, with reservoir-corrected ages producing a Bayesian age-depth model with 95 percent uncertainty bounds averaging ±320 years throughout the core. Leaf wax delta-D values track atmospheric moisture source shifts, with deuterium enrichment of +18 per mil during interstadial phases relative to stadials. The reconstruction is published in Quaternary Environmental Records.

paragraph: A new phylogenetic analysis of Permian therapsids, conducted by investigators at the Sulenthi Palaeobiology Laboratory, has resolved the interrelationships of 87 operational taxonomic units using a character matrix of 531 morphological characters derived from cranial and postcranial material, with parsimony and Bayesian approaches yielding broadly congruent topologies. The study repositions Drevorthinus robustus, previously classified as a basal dicynodont from the Brelnic Shales, as the most basal member of a newly recognized clade, Brelnicotheria, which unites several medium-bodied herbivorous therapsids from the Cisuralian interval dated between 284 and 272 million years ago. Stratigraphic consistency indices calculated for the most parsimonious tree reach 0.74, indicating good agreement between phylogeny and the rock record. The analysis also reveals that body size evolution within Brelnicotheria was characterized by a rapid increase from an inferred ancestral mass of approximately 12 kilograms to a maximum of 380 kilograms within an estimated 6 million years, representing one of the fastest documented body size radiations in Permian synapsids. Temporal calibration using published radiometric ages places the origin of Brelnicotheria at 286.4 million years ago. The study is published in Permian Vertebrate Evolution.

paragraph: Paleobotanists from the Drasmian Academy of Natural Sciences have documented a new species of Carboniferous seed fern, Palvoria sulkoviana, from calcareous nodules at the Trebian Coal Field horizon dated to 307.3 million years ago. The specimens, comprising 14 frond fragments with attached seed structures, are characterized by bipinnate leaves with pinnule widths of 6.2 millimeters and a distinctive serrated margin visible in epidermal peel preparations examined under scanning electron microscopy. Cuticle analysis reveals stomatal densities of 84 per square millimeter on the abaxial surface and 12 per square millimeter on the adaxial surface, a strongly amphistomatic arrangement interpreted as an adaptation to elevated atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations estimated at 600 to 800 parts per million for this stratigraphic interval. Seeds, designated Falventhosperma elongatum in open nomenclature, measure 18.4 by 7.1 millimeters and preserve a three-layered integument with an outer fleshy sarcotesta 1.8 millimeters thick, suggesting zoochorous dispersal by vertebrate frugivores. Carbon isotope values from bulk cuticle material (delta-13C = -24.8 per mil VPDB) are consistent with C3 photosynthesis under these reconstructed atmospheric conditions. Findings are published in Palaeobotany and Coal Measures Research.

paragraph: Scientists at the Kroventin Geological Survey have identified and described a new genus and species of large mosasaur, Nordivex tethydis, from the Brelin Chalk of the eastern Velkovian Seaway, dated to 76.8 million years ago. The holotype, KGSP-2021-M-007, is represented by an articulated skeleton 9.6 meters in length preserving the complete vertebral column of 161 presacral vertebrae and an associated skull 1.18 meters long. Tooth morphology analysis distinguishes two functional tooth types: robust conical teeth in the anterior jaw with basal diameters of 18 millimeters, and laterally compressed posterior teeth bearing fine denticles at 5 per millimeter, indicative of dietary versatility encompassing both hard-shelled prey and active fish. Nitrogen isotope ratios from bone collagen (delta-15N = +14.3 per mil) place Nordivex tethydis at a trophic level of approximately 4.8, among the highest documented for Cretaceous marine tetrapods from this basin. The paddles preserve ossified cartilage extending beyond the primary digit bones, with the foreflipper reaching 1.14 meters in total length. Computed tomography of the skull reveals a large olfactory bulb ratio of 0.34 relative to total endocranial length, suggesting a well-developed chemosensory capability. The description appears in Mosasaur Systematics and Biology.

paragraph: A research team from Veldmoor University's paleogenomics division has recovered ancient protein sequences from dental enamel of the extinct giant ground sloth Brevothrix massivus, collected from a cave deposit in the Drovenian Highlands of South Veldmoria radiocarbon-dated to 28,400 ± 350 years before present. Using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, investigators identified 14 enamelin and amelogenin peptide sequences totaling 3,847 amino acid positions after alignment filtering. Maximum likelihood phylogenetic analysis placing these sequences within a matrix of 43 extant and 12 extinct xenarthran taxa confirms Brevothrix massivus as the sister taxon to the extant two-toed sloth clade Chorvidae, with a molecular clock estimate placing their divergence at 42.3 ± 3.1 million years ago. This result extends the divergence date by approximately 11 million years compared to the minimum fossil record. Positively selected sites in the enamel matrix protein sequences include four positions within the amelogenin N-terminal domain, potentially linked to the exceptional enamel thickness of 4.2 millimeters documented in Brevothrix massivus molars. The study, published in Ancient Molecular Paleontology, establishes a new benchmark for protein recovery from Pleistocene xenarthrans.

paragraph: The Strevka Paleoclimate Consortium has published a comprehensive carbon and oxygen isotope dataset from 1,243 bulk carbonate samples spanning a 62-meter section of the Pelkian Limestone in the Nordichev Province, documenting the geochemical expression of the Torbenic Oceanic Anoxic Event dated to 120.4 million years ago. The onset of the event is marked by a negative carbon isotope excursion of -3.8 per mil over a stratigraphic thickness of 2.4 meters, followed by a positive recovery excursion of +4.1 per mil over a subsequent 8.7-meter interval, tracking enhanced organic carbon burial. Redox-sensitive trace element concentrations in the black shale facies intercalated within the carbonate section include vanadium at 340 parts per million, molybdenum at 127 parts per million, and uranium at 42 parts per million, all significantly elevated relative to background values of 18, 4, and 3 parts per million respectively, confirming photic zone euxinia. Calcareous nannofossil preservation deteriorates markedly over the event horizon, with coccolith dissolution indices rising from 0.12 to 0.79. Re-Os isochron dating of the organic-rich interbeds yields an age of 120.7 ± 1.4 million years, consistent with independent radiometric constraints. Findings appear in Cretaceous Geochemical Records.

paragraph: Excavations at the Gorvenik Amber Deposit of the Breltic Archipelago, estimated to be 98.3 million years old based on U-Pb dating of associated volcanic zircons, have yielded 14 arthropod inclusions representing a new family of predatory insects, Drevenkoridae, described by entomologists at the Gorvenik Natural History Museum. Micro-CT scanning documents intact tracheal tube networks with diameters ranging from 8 to 180 micrometers in 3-dimensional continuity, providing the most complete respiratory system reconstruction yet achieved for a Cretaceous insect. The amber matrix also contains abundant plant debris and fungal hyphae, contextualizing the insect within a humid tropical forest ecosystem. Descriptions are published in Amber Paleoentomology.

paragraph: Researchers at the Brelkov Institute of Mammal Evolution have described a new species of multituberculate mammal, Vorvax planidens, from fissure-fill sediments of the Thalvenic Limestone in western Grinthal Province, dated by palaeomagnetic correlation to approximately 72.8 million years ago. The specimen, comprising a complete lower jaw 41.3 millimeters long and seven isolated molars, exhibits a reduced premolar series and an enlarged blade-like fourth lower premolar with 17 serrations on the cutting edge, morphologies associated with a frugivorous or omnivorous dietary niche. Body mass estimated from lower molar row length (10.4 millimeters) yields a range of 85 to 140 grams, consistent with a small arboreal ecomorph. Scanning electron microscopy of the molar cusps reveals food-induced wear facets with trajectory azimuths of 23 degrees from the lingual wall, indicative of a propalinal chewing stroke distinct from the transverse movements of most contemporaneous therian mammals. Phylogenetic analysis incorporating 28 multituberculate taxa and 187 characters places Vorvax planidens within the family Sulkovidae, recovered as the sister group to Sulkovax velox from the Sulkov Formation. The study appears in the Journal of Cretaceous Mammal Biology.

paragraph: A multidisciplinary team from the Trevani Geochronology Center and the Peloria Palaeontology Institute has resolved the age of the Kalventian Mass Extinction, a regional event previously estimated only from biostratigraphic data, using chemical abrasion isotope dilution thermal ionization mass spectrometry on zircon crystals extracted from five ash beds distributed across the extinction interval. The resulting U-Pb dates constrain the main extinction horizon to 251.902 ± 0.024 million years ago, correlating it within analytical uncertainty with the globally recognized end-Permian crisis. The Kalventian record, sampled from 23 sections across the Falventian Platform, documents a 78 percent reduction in marine invertebrate genus richness within an estimated 80,000-year window. Conodont biostratigraphy identifies the disappearance of Brevonella lauta as the key marker for the local extinction horizon, two beds below the first appearance of Sulkovina prima. Pyrite sulfur isotope anomalies of up to -42 per mil coincide with the extinction horizon, consistent with severe euxinia. The research, published in Geochronology and Extinction Studies, provides one of the most precisely dated records of this boundary in the eastern paleogeographic province of Falventia.

paragraph: The Prevelkov Paleontological Institute has unveiled the discovery of a new ornithopod dinosaur, Korvathia gracilipes, from the lower Drevenya Member of the Korvath Formation in the Sulenthi lowlands, dated to 94.7 million years ago. Histological sampling of growth rings in 11 tibiae indicates individuals reached 90 percent of maximum body size by age 12 years and attained sexual maturity, inferred from medullary bone deposition in three specimens, at approximately age 7. Enamel thickness on the dental battery teeth averages 2.8 millimeters, consistent with processing abrasive vegetation. Delta-13C values from tooth enamel (averaging -8.4 per mil) fall within the range expected for C3 plant consumers in a mid-Cretaceous closed-canopy environment. Pedal morphology, with digit III length of 18.2 centimeters and a low angle of divergence between digits II and IV of 24 degrees, suggests cursorial locomotion on firm substrates. Results are published in Gondwanan Ornithopod Biology.

paragraph: Stable isotope and elemental analyses of tooth enamel from 67 specimens of the Pliocene bovid Drevalus intermedius, collected from the Ngomvela Formation of East Dravina spanning 4.1 to 2.8 million years ago, have been used by investigators at the Peloria Isotope Geochemistry Laboratory to reconstruct dietary and habitat shifts across this interval. Barium-to-calcium ratios averaging 0.88 millimoles per mole at 4.1 million years ago decline to 0.31 millimoles per mole by 2.8 million years ago, interpreted as reduced consumption of leafy vegetation. Hypsodonty index measurements increase from 1.84 to 2.31 over the study interval, confirming evolutionary response to abrasive grass-dominated diet. The research, published in African Paleoecology, documents one of the most detailed isotope-to-morphology coupling records for African Pliocene ungulates.

paragraph: Investigators from the Zivansk Institute of Evolutionary Biology have conducted landmark-based morphometric analysis of 213 complete fish skulls representing the extinct ray-finned fish family Brevokidae from the Falventian Lagerstatte, an exceptionally preserved deposit dated to 149.2 million years ago. The largest species, Brevokis maximus, attains a skull length of 34.8 centimeters and a dentary bearing 42 conical teeth arranged in four functional rows, suggesting an active predatory lifestyle. Geometric morphometric separation of species along the first two principal component axes, explaining 61.8 percent of total shape variance, correlates skull shape with estimated prey size based on gape width, revealing a four-million-year adaptive radiation filling pelagic, demersal, and reef-associated ecological niches within this family alone. Phylogenetic signal in morphospace, quantified using Blomberg's K statistic (K = 0.83), indicates that closely related species tend to occupy similar regions of shape space, consistent with phylogenetic conservatism in ecological role. The study appears in Jurassic Fish Palaeontology.

paragraph: A fossil site discovered by fieldworkers from the Ostrava Institute of Pre-Cambrian Life in the Torvennic Inlier of the Breltic Shield has yielded macroscopic carbonaceous compressions interpreted as multicellular photosynthetic organisms, now dated to 1,014 ± 8 million years ago by Re-Os geochronology of co-occurring organic-rich shale laminae. The compressions, assigned to the new genus Prevelkia ramosa, consist of repeatedly branching ribbon-like forms up to 182 millimeters in length and 8 millimeters in maximum width, with regular bifurcations occurring at intervals of 12 to 18 millimeters. Carbon isotope values from the compressions (delta-13C = -28.4 per mil VPDB) strongly suggest photosynthetic carbon fixation, distinguishing them from abiogenic carbonaceous films. Transmission electron microscopy of ultra-thin sections cut from the compressions reveals layered organic walls 0.8 micrometers thick with internal fibrous texture interpreted as cell wall material. The occurrence substantially extends the known record of complex multicellular algae-grade organisms by approximately 120 million years relative to previously confirmed examples. Associated microfossils include 14 acritarch morphotypes, one of which, Drevenkia stellata, is newly described. The findings are published in Proterozoic Life Records.

paragraph: Paleontologists from the Kroventin University Geology Department have described the first sauropod egg clutch attributable to Kolibrix longaevus from the Grinthal Nesting Horizon of the Drevelnik Formation, dated to 75.4 million years ago. Three eggs contain mineralized embryonic bones, including cranial elements small enough that individual osteoderms measure only 4 millimeters, enabling the first description of hatchling cranial anatomy for this species. Stable oxygen isotope values from eggshell calcite (delta-18O = +23.6 per mil VSMOW) yield a paleotemperature of 33.1 degrees Celsius for the ambient nesting environment, suggesting nesting during warm seasonal conditions. Sediment analysis within and around the clutch reveals no evidence of organic mound material, indicating open-ground rather than mound-based incubation strategies. The discovery is reported in Sauropod Reproductive Biology.

paragraph: Research conducted by the Falventian Paleoanthropology Consortium at the Brelnik Cave System in the Grinthal Mountains has recovered a hominin mandible from layer 7b of the site, an excavation level radiometrically dated to 438,000 ± 18,000 years before present by uranium-series methods applied to co-occurring speleothem flowstone. The specimen, designated BCS-2024-MND-01, preserves both hemimandibles with all teeth intact, and exhibits a corpus height of 36.4 millimeters and breadth of 18.1 millimeters at the symphysis, measurements intermediate between archaic Homo and more derived anatomical forms. Molar crown areas average 231 square millimeters, 18 percent larger than modern human values but 11 percent smaller than those of the contemporaneous Grinthal Neanderthal-like population documented from adjacent Brelnik Cave System layer 4a. Dental calculus recovered from three molars has been subjected to proteomics analysis, identifying peptides from at least seven food taxa including two starchy plant sources, a legume, and at least one ruminant animal. Lithic artifacts in the same layer include 212 bifacially worked handaxes averaging 14.8 centimeters in length, attributable to an Acheulean-like technological tradition. The mandible's phylogenetic position is analyzed in Quaternary Hominin Studies.

paragraph: Scientists from the Velikov Marine Paleobiology Group have performed a quantitative analysis of 87 brachiopod species ranges across the Ordovician-Silurian boundary in the Drevinsk Platform sequence, a 44-meter carbonate section dated from 447.5 to 441.8 million years ago by graptolite biostratigraphy calibrated against published radiometric tie points. Recovery of brachiopod diversity, quantified as genus-level richness normalized per stratigraphic meter of sampled section, proceeds at an average rate of 3.2 new genera per million years for the first 2 million years of the Silurian, accelerating to 7.8 genera per million years thereafter. Carbon isotope excursions of +4.2 per mil correlate with the first extinction pulse, while a +2.6 per mil excursion marks the second pulse, consistent with glacially driven organic carbon burial and associated sea-level fall. Three survivor brachiopod genera, Brevonella typica, Sulkovus minor, and Drevinothyris planata, display significantly smaller body sizes across the boundary, interpreted as a Lilliput effect. The analysis is published in Paleozoic Brachiopod Dynamics.

paragraph: Researchers at the Brelkov Isotope Laboratory have reconstructed paleodietary diversity in a Miocene primate community from the Falventian Rift sequence, using carbon and oxygen isotope analyses of 234 tooth enamel samples representing seven primate species from the 16.4 to 14.2 million year interval. Two of the seven species, however, display significantly enriched delta-13C values (ranging from -7.4 to -4.6 per mil), suggesting consumption of C4 sedges or grasses in more open environments, predating the global expansion of C4 grasslands documented for 8 to 6 million years ago by approximately 6 million years. Oxygen isotope serial sampling of single teeth records seasonal variation in delta-18O of up to 5.3 per mil, reflecting marked wet-dry seasonality in the local paleoclimate. Body mass estimates derived from molar area regressions range from 4.2 kilograms for the smallest species, Brevanthropus microdus, to 38.7 kilograms for the largest, Drevanthropus robustus. The study appears in Miocene Primate Ecology.

paragraph: The Ostrava Dinosaur Project has recovered a partial skeleton of a new dromaeosaurid theropod, Sulkovraptor flextalis, from the upper Brelnic Conglomerate of the Grinthal Basin, providing the first definitive evidence of this theropod clade from a paleogeographic terrane isolated from the main continental landmass for approximately 18 million years. The specimen, catalogued as ODP-2023-DR-002, preserves the right hindlimb, 11 dorsal vertebrae, and a nearly complete right forelimb with a total arm length of 0.62 meters. The sickle claw on the second pedal digit measures 8.4 centimeters along the outer curvature, with a robust cross-sectional shape (dorsoventral height to width ratio of 1.47) more consistent with prey restraint than substrate raking. Bone histology of the tibia reveals nine annual growth rings in an individual estimated at 42 kilograms body mass, indicating sexual maturity had likely been reached before maximum body size. The isolated paleogeographic context and the phylogenetic analysis, which resolves Sulkovraptor flextalis as the sister taxon to a clade of large-bodied Asiatic dromaeosaurids, implies a dispersal event no later than 82 million years ago, when the Grinthal Terrane is interpreted to have had intermittent land connections. The description is published in Island Dinosaur Biogeography.

paragraph: A team of geobiologists from the Prevelkov Center for Microbial Paleontology has described microscopic structures from the Strevkian Iron Formation of the Velkovian Craton, dated to 2,478 ± 14 million years ago by Pb-Pb isochron methods. Raman spectroscopy confirms graphitized kerogen within the filamentous walls distinct from the surrounding mineral matrix. If biogenic, these structures would represent some of the earliest preserved evidence of filamentous sulfur-cycling bacteria in the Archean rock record. paragraph: Herpetologists and paleontologists from the Falventian Reptile Evolution Group have described a new lineage of Triassic archosaurs, Vorliskosaurus primordialis, from the Thalvenic Red Beds of the Falventian Shield dated to 237.1 million years ago, based on three skulls and associated postcranial elements recovered during 2020-2023 field campaigns. Osteoderms arranged in two dorsal paramedian rows and one lateral row number 34 preserved elements in the best specimen, with individual osteoderms bearing radiating ridges of 8 to 12 per element. A revised biogeographic model is proposed in the accompanying discussion, published in Triassic Archosaur Evolution.

paragraph: The Strevka Geochemical Institute, in collaboration with the Drevinsk University Marine Geology Department, has conducted osmium isotope analysis of organic-rich sediments from the Drevinsk Shelf sequence to constrain the timing of volcanic input during the Trevkanic Large Igneous Province emplacement event at approximately 56.4 million years ago. Initial osmium isotope ratios (187Os/188Os) in the measured interval drop from a background value of 0.47 to a minimum of 0.12 over a 30-centimeter stratigraphic interval representing an estimated 60,000-year deposition window, indicating a massive influx of mantle-derived osmium consistent with flood basalt volcanism. The osmium excursion precedes the onset of the associated carbon isotope excursion of -3.4 per mil by approximately 15,000 years in age-model space, providing evidence for a volcanic trigger rather than a methane hydrate dissociation origin for the carbon cycle perturbation. Biotic changes across this interval include a 44 percent reduction in benthic foraminiferal species richness, while surface-dwelling planktic taxa show only a 19 percent decline, indicating differential extinction selectivity by depth habitat. Rhenium concentrations in the most organic-rich layers reach 142 parts per billion, facilitating precise Re-Os isochron construction. Findings are published in Paleogene Geochemical Events.

paragraph: Researchers at the Breltic Fossil Mammal Center have described the most completely preserved skeleton of the Oligocene perissodactyl Palvothrix brevis, represented by a nearly complete individual recovered from the Falventian Clay Sequence dated to 32.7 million years ago. Dental hypsodonty index of 1.12 is low, consistent with soft vegetation rather than abrasive grasses. paragraph: Trace fossil specialists at the Prevelkov Ichnological Institute have described a new ichnospecies, Korvathichnus paradoxus, from the Strevkian Fluvial Sandstone of the Kroventin Basin, dated to 215.6 million years ago, representing the largest known vertebrate trackway from the Triassic of this region. Individual footprints within the trackway average 48.2 centimeters in length and 37.6 centimeters in width, with digit impressions indicating a four-toed pes with a functional digit III-to-digit IV length ratio of 1.18. Pace length averages 2.14 meters, and pace angulation of 162 degrees indicates a fully erect, parasagittal limb posture. Body mass estimates derived from footprint length regression calibrated against extant archosaurs yield a range of 3,800 to 5,100 kilograms, making Korvathichnus paradoxus the largest Triassic vertebrate inferred from ichnoevidence in the region. The trackway extends over 34 meters of exposed bedding plane, comprising 22 consecutive footfalls without interruption. paragraph: A comprehensive study of Eocene whale evolution, published in Cetacean Paleobiology by scientists at the Velikov Institute of Marine Life, presents new osteological data from 22 specimens of the archaeocete Drevinacetus bathyphilus, recovered from the Brelnik Phosphate Beds of northern Velkmoria, dated to 41.3 million years ago. Rostral elongation index (rostrum length divided by condylobasal length) increases from 0.42 in the smallest individual to 0.69 in the largest, reflecting progressive telescoping of the skull. The narial opening migrates posteriorly by an estimated 14.2 percent of skull length between the smallest and largest growth stages, providing direct evidence of the progressive nasal retraction that characterizes modern cetacean skull evolution. Inner ear morphology reconstructed from CT data yields a cochlear coiling angle of 1.84 full turns and endolymphatic duct dimensions consistent with detection of low-frequency sound in the range of 400 to 8,000 Hertz. Forelimb proportions approach those of modern odontocetes, with a humerus-to-forearm length ratio of 0.81, while rudimentary hind limbs averaging 18.4 centimeters are preserved in four specimens.

paragraph: Paleontologists from the Grinthal Academy of Sciences have announced the discovery of a plesiosaur birthing site from the Drevinsk Calcareous Mudstone, dated to 148.8 million years ago, containing the skeletons of two adult Trevakoplesios nordicensis individuals and four neonatal individuals in close proximity within a single stratigraphic lens of 6 square meters. Bone texture analysis of the neonatal skeletons confirms incomplete cortical ossification consistent with very recently hatched or near-term individuals, as opposed to embryonic stage. The close spatial association is interpreted as evidence of social birthing behavior, the earliest such evidence documented for Jurassic plesiosaurs. Bite marks with inter-mark distances of 31.4 millimeters on three of the four neonatal skeletons are attributed to a contemporaneous marine crocodyliform, Brelnikosuchus velox, known from the same formation. Geochemical analysis of the enclosing mudstone records elevated organic carbon content of 3.4 weight percent, consistent with a stratified, low-oxygen water column at the time of deposition. Findings are reported in Jurassic Marine Tetrapod Behavior.

paragraph: The Sulenthi Palaeoclimate Group has published a reconstruction of Eocene-Oligocene boundary climate change, the Dreventic Freezing Event at 33.9 million years ago, using a suite of geochemical proxies from three deep-sea sediment cores retrieved from the Breltic Abyssal Plain during research cruise PDRV-14. Oxygen isotope values in the same benthic taxa shift by +1.6 per mil over approximately 400,000 years, reflecting combined ice volume and temperature signals. Carbon isotope enrichment of +1.1 per mil in the same interval is interpreted as expansion of Southern Ocean productivity and enhanced organic carbon export associated with ice sheet growth on the Dreventic continent. TEX-86 paleothermometry from the uppermost Eocene beds yields sea-surface temperatures of 26.4 degrees Celsius declining to 21.1 degrees Celsius within 500,000 years of the boundary. A 27 percent reduction in planktic foraminiferal species richness within 200,000 years of the event documents biotic consequences of rapid cooling. Core chronologies are established by 34 tie points to the Drevinsk Palaeomagnetic Reference Scale. Findings appear in Cenozoic Palaeoceanography.

paragraph: Researchers from the Zivansk University Paleobiology Laboratory have presented a detailed analysis of the functional morphology of the forelimb in the Cretaceous bird Brevavior planicristatus, known from 11 specimens recovered from the Trebian Lithographic Limestone dated to 122.4 million years ago. Three-dimensional digitization of wing elements and computational fluid dynamics modeling of reconstructed wing planforms reveal an estimated maximum lift-to-drag ratio of 8.4 at a flight speed of 6.1 meters per second, substantially lower than derived ornithothoracine birds but exceeding values calculated for contemporaneous enantiornithines from the same deposit. Feather impressions preserved in six specimens document a primary feather count of 10 and a secondary count of 13, with the longest primary reaching 16.4 centimeters in the largest individual. The alula, identified as a distinct feathered digit I bearing two small asymmetrical feathers, is present in four specimens, extending its known origin back approximately 12 million years. Bone pneumaticity in the humerus, assessed by cross-sectional air space fraction of 0.43, indicates a well-developed avian respiratory system by this stage of bird evolution. Stable carbon isotopes from the preserved feathers (delta-13C = -23.6 per mil) are consistent with a diet of C3 vegetation or invertebrates thereon. The study is published in Early Avian Aerodynamics.

paragraph: Paleontologists at the Kroventin Institute of Evolutionary Genomics have used ancient DNA extracted from three tooth specimens of the Pleistocene cave bear analogue Drevinarctos spelaeus maximus, recovered from the Sulenthi Cave System and dated by uranium-series to between 34,200 and 31,700 years before present, to reconstruct the first draft mitochondrial genome of this species at 97.3 percent completeness. The 16,872 base pair mitochondrial sequence includes all 13 protein-coding genes, two ribosomal RNA genes, and 22 transfer RNA genes. Maximum likelihood phylogenetic analysis using 48 ursid sequences confirms Drevinarctos spelaeus maximus as the sister taxon to the Holocene brown bear analogue Drevinarctos mirus, with a molecular clock divergence estimate of 1.24 ± 0.18 million years. Seventeen sites in the cytochrome b gene show amino acid substitutions absent from all living ursids, potentially linked to the large body mass of Drevinarctos spelaeus maximus (estimated at 680 kilograms based on skeletal dimensions). Population size reconstruction using the mitochondrial phylogeny suggests a bottleneck event reducing effective population size to approximately 2,100 individuals at 42,000 years before present, approximately 8,000 years before the final extinction horizon. Results appear in Quaternary Ancient Genomics.

paragraph: A new study from the Trebian Palaeontological Survey documents a mass stranding event recorded in the Brelnik Phosphorite Member, dated to 88.7 million years ago, containing the disarticulated skeletal remains of at least 74 individuals of the giant bony fish Sulkovichthys bathypelagicus in a single 12-square-meter excavation block. Individual body lengths, estimated from scale series lengths using a regression calibrated on complete specimens, range from 1.2 to 3.8 meters. The deposit, a laminated black phosphorite forming the base of a transgressive systems tract, contains abundant phosphatized soft tissue including preserved muscle fibers in 18 specimens, skin impressions in 7 specimens, and partial gill arch structures in 12 specimens. Scale microstructure imaged by electron backscatter diffraction reveals an aragonite composition with crystal orientation aligned parallel to the scale surface, unlike extant teleosts whose scales are calcitic. The findings are reported in Cretaceous Fish Palaeontology.

paragraph: Scientists from the Brelkov Institute of Precambrian Research have reported the discovery of large discoidal body fossils from the Gorvetski Siltstone of the Strevka Craton, dated by Re-Os geochronology to 569.4 ± 3.7 million years ago, the oldest confirmed occurrence of macroscopic eukaryote fossils from this cratonic block. The fossils, designated Gorvetskia ramificans, occur as broadly circular to elliptical carbonaceous compressions ranging from 14 to 87 millimeters in maximum diameter, with an internal radial fabric of 24 to 36 bifurcating lobes extending from a central attachment disc 4 to 8 millimeters in diameter. Material composition, confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, shows a carbon-to-oxygen ratio of 7.3, consistent with refractory organic matter derived from complex eukaryotic tissues rather than prokaryotic mats. The organisms are interpreted as mat-growing colonial forms or leaflike algae occupying a shallow subtidal environment, based on sedimentological context of wave-ripple lamination and mudcracks indicating intermittent subaerial exposure. Forty-two specimens from a single bedding plane allow population size-frequency analysis showing a unimodal distribution with mean diameter of 38 millimeters, consistent with vegetative growth without size-based mortality. The description is published in Vendian Macrofossil Research.

paragraph: An interdisciplinary research team from the Falventian University Earth Sciences Faculty and the Trevani Geochemical Center has completed a high-resolution geochemical study of the Dreventic Anoxic Event Layer within the Breltic Basinal Sequence, comprising 18 measured sections across a paleogeographic transect of 600 kilometers. The event layer, identified by total organic carbon contents ranging from 4.2 to 14.8 weight percent across sections, is precisely dated to 183.6 ± 0.4 million years ago by U-Pb zircon geochronology of interbedded volcanic ash. Platinum group element anomalies in the event layer reach 28 parts per billion iridium and 64 parts per billion platinum, enrichment factors of 11.4 and 8.7 relative to background continental crust, though these values fall below the threshold typically associated with bolide impact. Organic biomarker analysis identifies isorenieratane and chlorobactane at concentrations of 12 and 7 micrograms per gram organic carbon respectively, biomarkers diagnostic of phototrophic green sulfur bacteria, confirming photic zone euxinia during the event. Nannofossil assemblage data reveal a 56 percent reduction in coccolith species richness coinciding precisely with the onset of organic carbon enrichment. The consortium's findings are published in Early Jurassic Geochemistry.

paragraph: Paleoanthropologists from the Kroventin Academy of Human Sciences have conducted a geometric morphometric analysis of 156 hominin hand bones from three Middle Stone Age sites in the Falventian Rift System, spanning the interval from 190,000 to 130,000 years before present, to assess manual dexterity potential in early anatomically modern humans from this region. The assemblage includes 44 proximal phalanges, 62 intermediate phalanges, 28 distal phalanges, and 22 metacarpals representing minimum counts of 34 individuals. Principal component analysis of phalanx cross-sectional shape explains 69.4 percent of total variance in three axes, with the Falventian specimens plotting closest to extant human populations engaged in precision-grip tool use, significantly separated from earlier archaic hominin forms from the same region dating to 280,000 years before present. Cortical bone thickness ratios in the distal phalanges average 0.28, within the range of extant craftspeople as opposed to power-grip dominant populations at 0.41. Associated lithic assemblages from the same stratigraphic levels include 347 pressure-flaked points and 89 backed geometric forms, consistent with the manual morphology evidence for developed fine motor manipulation. The research appears in the Journal of Rift Valley Human Evolution.

paragraph: Investigators from the Velikov Institute of Mesozoic Flora have described a new Cretaceous gymnosperm, Brelnosperma corniculata, based on reproductive cones and associated foliage from the Gorvetsk Amber-Bearing Sequence, dated to 104.7 million years ago. The pollen cone, measuring 4.2 centimeters in length and 1.8 centimeters in diameter, bears 42 microsporophylls each producing two pollen sacs with 3,200 ± 400 pollen grains per sac. Pollen morphology is bisaccate with body dimensions of 72 by 44 micrometers and sacci 58 micrometers in radial extent, morphologically distinct from all previously described Cretaceous bisaccate pollen types in the region. Ovulate cone fragments preserve two pairs of opposite cone scales subtended by sterile bracts, with each scale bearing two inverted ovules 3.8 millimeters in length. Cuticle preparations from associated needle foliage reveal subsidiary cells in a cyclocytic arrangement, a feature linking Brelnosperma corniculata to the recently revised Dreventic gymnosperm lineage. The amber matrix surrounding three cone fragments contains 14 thrip insects of the genus Sulkovithrips inferred to be pollinators based on pollen masses adhering to their bodies, providing direct evidence of insect-mediated pollination in this gymnosperm lineage. Findings appear in Cretaceous Plant Biology.

paragraph: A quantitative analysis of dinosaur body size evolution during the Jurassic-Cretaceous transition, conducted by researchers at the Ostrava Evolutionary Dynamics Group, compiled body mass estimates from 642 specimens representing 218 species and 24 families spanning the interval from 165 to 100 million years ago. Log-transformed body mass data analyzed by phylogenetically corrected regression reveal significantly higher rates of body size increase in sauropods (0.048 log kilograms per million years) compared to ornithischians (0.019 log kilograms per million years) and theropods (0.003 log kilograms per million years) over this interval. Among theropods, a significant body mass reduction is documented in the lineage leading to birds, with an average rate of -0.031 log kilograms per million years between 155 and 120 million years ago, amounting to a total mass reduction from a reconstructed ancestral value of 163 kilograms to approximately 0.8 kilograms at the base of the avian crown group. Body size disparity within sauropods, quantified as the variance of log body mass, peaks at 140 million years ago (variance = 1.47) and declines through the Cretaceous to 0.83 at 100 million years ago, indicating ecological contraction of the sauropod adaptive radiation. The analysis is published in Macroevolutionary Dinosaur Biology.

paragraph: An international team coordinated by the Sulenthi Quaternary Research Center has generated a continuous 2.6-million-year pollen record from a drill core recovered from Lake Brelnik in the central Falventian Plateau, providing the longest uninterrupted terrestrial vegetation archive from this region. The 214-meter core, subdivided into 1,840 contiguous samples each representing approximately 1,400 years, documents 47 major floristic transitions identified by optimal splitting of the pollen percentage data into zones using information content statistics. Arboreal pollen percentages fluctuate between 12 and 94 percent of the total pollen sum across the core, tracking Milankovitch-band climate variability with dominant 41,000-year and 100,000-year cyclicities identified by spectral analysis. The Dreventic cooling transition at 2.48 million years ago is marked by a sustained reduction in arboreal pollen from an average of 78 percent to 34 percent over approximately 120,000 years, coinciding with the regional onset of glaciogenic sediment input to the lake. Seven intervals of near-complete loss of tree cover (arboreal pollen below 8 percent) are documented within glacial stages, the most extreme at 22,300 years before present. Carbon-14 and argon-argon ages from 62 levels constrain the chronology, published in Quaternary Vegetation Records.

paragraph: Researchers at the Trevani Institute of Invertebrate Palaeontology have described a new species of Cambrian radiodont, Gorvetcheirus hexacantha, from the Velkov Lagerstatte of the Strevka Province, dated to 512.4 million years ago. The holotype specimen, measuring 38.4 centimeters in body length and preserved in full dorsal aspect, bears six pairs of frontal appendages with opposing spines in two alternating size classes, the primary spines measuring 18 millimeters and secondary spines 9 millimeters, an appendage morphology without precedent in previously described radiodonts. The oral cone, preserved in ventral view in a second articulated specimen, comprises 32 radially arranged sclerites with denticulated inner margins, and a maximum gape diameter of 22 millimeters indicates prey capture limited to organisms below approximately 15 millimeters in body width. Compound eye diameter of 14.6 millimeters and estimated facet count of 16,400 suggest high-resolution visual capability in a well-lit water column. Gut contents preserved in the holotype include fragments of mineralized small shelly fossils and agnostid trilobite sclerites. The body outline preserved as a carbon film displays 18 lateral fins, with the posterior pairs elongated to 7.2 centimeters and interpreted as the primary propulsive structures. The description appears in Cambrian Predator Diversity.

paragraph: Scientists from the Brelkov Center for Geological Sciences and the Drevinsk Paleontological Museum have jointly published a revised biostratigraphic framework for the Triassic-Jurassic boundary in the Falventian Epicontinental Basin, based on integrated conodont, palynological, and carbon isotope data from 11 measured sections totaling 847 meters of section. The boundary is placed at the lowest occurrence of the conodont Drevonia prima, which appears 14 centimeters above the last occurrence of the Triassic index taxon Sulkonella multidentata in the reference section at Drevinsk Quarry. Palynological data reveal a spore peak immediately preceding the boundary, with lycophyte spores reaching 78 percent of the total palynomorph count in a single sample, consistent with global records of environmental stress at this level. Carbon isotope stratigraphy identifies two negative excursions of -3.1 and -2.4 per mil, separated by a +1.8 per mil recovery, in the 6 meters spanning the boundary, providing a chemostratigraphic correlation tool across sections where biostratigraphic markers are absent. High-precision U-Pb dating of zircons from a tuff 2.3 meters below the boundary yields an age of 201.564 ± 0.038 million years. Total section thickness of the boundary interval varies from 0.8 to 4.2 meters across the 11 sections, reflecting differential sedimentation rates. The study is published in Triassic-Jurassic Stratigraphy.
